text:
COMPREHENSIVE GENOMIC PROFILING REPORT
Next-Generation Sequencing — Solid Tumor Panel

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
PATIENT & SPECIMEN INFORMATION
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Patient Name:             Margaret Okonkwo
Date of Birth:            [on file]
Sex:                      Female
MRN:                      [on file]
Ordering Physician:       Head and Neck Medical Oncology / Multidisciplinary Team
Diagnosis:                Oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, HPV-positive (p16+), base of tongue
Clinical Stage:           T2N1M0, Stage I (AJCC 8th Ed., HPV-positive oropharynx)

Specimen ID:              [LAB-HNSCC-XXXXXX]
Specimen Type:            Formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue
Procedure:                Directed biopsy, left base of tongue (flexible fiberoptic laryngoscopy, clinic-based)
Tumor Cellularity:        Estimated 55% (reviewed by board-certified pathologist prior to DNA extraction)
Nucleic Acid Quality:     Sufficient for analysis; DIN 6.2 (acceptable for FFPE-derived specimen)

Panel:                    Comprehensive 500-gene targeted next-generation sequencing panel
Platform:                 Hybrid capture-based NGS, paired tumor/normal analysis (normal: peripheral blood)
Coverage:                 Mean on-target coverage: 742x (tumor); 312x (normal)
Analysis:                 Somatic SNVs, indels, copy number variations (CNVs), gene fusions, TMB, MSI
HPV Typing:               High-risk HPV detection performed as part of panel (RNA ISH confirmatory)
PD-L1:                    Reported separately (IHC, external send-out)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
SUMMARY OF FINDINGS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Pathogenic or Likely Pathogenic Somatic Alterations Detected:

  1. PIK3CA p.E545K (c.1633G>A), Exon 9 — POTENTIALLY ACTIONABLE
  2. TRAF3 p.R118* (c.352C>T), Exon 4 — BIOLOGICALLY RELEVANT
  3. KMT2D (MLL2) p.Q3382* (c.10144C>T), Exon 39 — BIOLOGICALLY RELEVANT
  4. EP300 p.R418C (c.1252C>T), Exon 8 — VARIANT OF UNCERTAIN SIGNIFICANCE (VUS, likely deleterious)
  5. FBXW7 p.R505C (c.1513C>T), Exon 9 — VARIANT OF UNCERTAIN SIGNIFICANCE (loss-of-function context)

Copy Number Alterations:
  - Focal gain of chromosome 3q (includes PIK3CA, SOX2, TP63 loci) — DETECTED
  - No CDKN2A/2B homozygous deletion detected
  - No EGFR amplification
  - No FGFR1 amplification
  - No MYC amplification

Gene Fusions / Structural Variants:
  - No actionable gene fusions detected
  - HPV-8q24 chromosomal integration site identified (see HPV section)

Biomarkers:
  - Tumor Mutational Burden (TMB): 4 mutations/Mb — LOW
  - Microsatellite Instability (MSI): Microsatellite Stable (MSS)
  - PD-L1 Combined Positive Score (CPS): 18 (by external IHC send-out, reported separately)
  - HPV Status: HIGH-RISK HPV DETECTED — HPV-16 (see below)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
DETAILED SOMATIC VARIANT FINDINGS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1. PIK3CA p.E545K — EXON 9 HELICAL DOMAIN HOTSPOT MUTATION
   Gene:                PIK3CA (phosphatidylinositol-4,5-bisphosphate 3-kinase catalytic subunit alpha)
   Variant:             c.1633G>A; p.Glu545Lys
   Variant Type:        Missense, activating
   Variant Allele Freq: 18% (tumor); 0% (germline/normal blood) — confirmed somatic
   Coverage:            Tumor: 814x; Variant reads: 147
   Classification:      Pathogenic — established gain-of-function hotspot
   Chromosomal Context: Co-localizes with focal 3q gain (PIK3CA locus at 3q26.32); possible gene dosage contribution
   Functional Impact:   p.E545K is a well-characterized activating mutation in the helical domain of PIK3CAalpha,
                        resulting in constitutive PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway activation independent of upstream
                        receptor tyrosine kinase signaling. This hotspot is frequently identified in HPV-positive
                        oropharyngeal SCC, occurring in approximately 25-35% of cases. The concomitant focal 3q
                        gain may increase PIK3CA copy number, augmenting oncogenic signaling.
   Clinical Relevance:  POTENTIALLY ACTIONABLE. PI3K pathway inhibitors (e.g., alpelisib, copanlisib) have
                        demonstrated preclinical and early clinical activity in PIK3CA-mutant head and neck SCC.
                        Not currently standard of care in the recurrence-naive setting; relevant if systemic
                        therapy is required for recurrent/metastatic disease. NCCN category 2B consideration.
                        MATCH/TAPUR basket trial eligibility may apply if recurrence occurs.
   Associated Pathways: PI3K-AKT-mTOR signaling

2. TRAF3 p.R118* — LOSS-OF-FUNCTION MUTATION
   Gene:                TRAF3 (TNF receptor-associated factor 3)
   Variant:             c.352C>T; p.Arg118Ter (stop gain)
   Variant Type:        Nonsense, loss-of-function
   Variant Allele Freq: 22% (tumor); 0% (normal) — confirmed somatic
   Coverage:            Tumor: 692x; Variant reads: 152
   Classification:      Likely Pathogenic (tumor suppressor, truncating mutation)
   Functional Impact:   TRAF3 encodes a tumor suppressor involved in innate immune signaling, type I interferon
                        production, and regulation of the non-canonical NF-kappaB pathway. Loss-of-function
                        mutations in TRAF3 lead to constitutive non-canonical NF-kappaB activation, which promotes
                        cell survival, proliferation, and immune evasion. TRAF3 is among the most frequently mutated
                        genes in HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC (reported in ~15-20% of cases), in contrast to its
                        infrequent alteration in HPV-negative HNSCC. Loss of TRAF3 in HPV-associated carcinogenesis
                        may cooperate with viral oncoproteins (E6/E7) to drive malignant transformation.
   Clinical Relevance:  Biologically relevant; not currently directly targetable. May have implications for
                        immune checkpoint inhibitor responsiveness (NF-kappaB pathway activation can modulate
                        tumor immunogenicity). No immediate therapeutic actionability in the curative-intent setting.
   Associated Pathways: NF-kappaB (non-canonical), innate immune signaling, type I IFN pathway

3. KMT2D (MLL2) p.Q3382* — TRUNCATING MUTATION
   Gene:                KMT2D (lysine methyltransferase 2D; also known as MLL4, MLL2)
   Variant:             c.10144C>T; p.Gln3382Ter
   Variant Type:        Nonsense, truncating
   Variant Allele Freq: 19% (tumor); 0% (normal) — confirmed somatic
   Coverage:            Tumor: 728x; Variant reads: 138
   Classification:      Likely Pathogenic (tumor suppressor context)
   Functional Impact:   KMT2D encodes a histone H3K4 methyltransferase that functions as a master regulator of
                        enhancer activation and gene expression. As a member of the COMPASS-like complex, KMT2D
                        establishes H3K4me1 marks at active enhancers, regulating the transcription of tumor
                        suppressor genes and differentiation programs. Truncating mutations in KMT2D are among the
                        most common epigenetic alterations across all HNSCC subtypes (both HPV-positive and
                        HPV-negative), occurring in approximately 15-20% of cases. Loss of KMT2D function leads
                        to widespread enhancer dysregulation and may contribute to dedifferentiation, increased
                        proliferative capacity, and resistance to certain therapeutic modalities.
   Clinical Relevance:  No direct targeted therapy currently approved. May have implications for sensitivity to
                        histone deacetylase (HDAC) inhibitors or EZH2 inhibitors in synthetic lethality
                        frameworks; this remains investigational. Not directly actionable in standard clinical
                        practice at this time.
   Associated Pathways: Chromatin remodeling, histone modification (H3K4 methylation), enhancer regulation

4. EP300 p.R418C — CHROMATIN REMODELING, VARIANT OF UNCERTAIN SIGNIFICANCE
   Gene:                EP300 (E1A binding protein p300; transcriptional coactivator / histone acetyltransferase)
   Variant:             c.1252C>T; p.Arg418Cys
   Variant Type:        Missense
   Variant Allele Freq: 15% (tumor); 0% (normal) — confirmed somatic
   Coverage:            Tumor: 661x; Variant reads: 99
   Classification:      Variant of Uncertain Significance (VUS), potentially deleterious
   Functional Impact:   EP300 encodes a transcriptional coactivator with intrinsic histone acetyltransferase (HAT)
                        activity, functioning broadly as a tumor suppressor and transcriptional integrator. p300
                        acetylates histone H3K27 at enhancers and cooperates with CBP in regulating differentiation
                        and apoptosis. The p.R418C substitution occurs within the zinc finger / TAZ1 domain. While
                        EP300 truncating mutations are frequently pathogenic in HNSCC, this missense variant at
                        p.R418C has limited functional characterization to date. Given the tumor suppressor role of
                        EP300 and the missense context affecting a conserved residue, this variant is classified as
                        a VUS with possible functional impact; in silico predictions (SIFT: Deleterious, PolyPhen-2:
                        Probably Damaging, CADD score: 28.4) support potential pathogenicity.
   Clinical Relevance:  Not directly actionable. May co-participate with KMT2D loss in an epigenetic dysregulation
                        landscape common to HNSCC. Continued monitoring of functional significance in the literature
                        is recommended.
   Associated Pathways: Transcriptional coactivation, histone acetyltransferase activity, p53 acetylation

5. FBXW7 p.R505C — UBIQUITIN LIGASE SUBSTRATE ADAPTOR, VUS
   Gene:                FBXW7 (F-box and WD repeat domain containing 7)
   Variant:             c.1513C>T; p.Arg505Cys
   Variant Type:        Missense; occurs at a known mutational hotspot residue (R465, R479, R505 — WD40 substrate
                        recognition domain hotspots)
   Variant Allele Freq: 12% (tumor); 0% (normal) — confirmed somatic
   Coverage:            Tumor: 583x; Variant reads: 70
   Classification:      Variant of Uncertain Significance, likely loss-of-function (hotspot context)
   Functional Impact:   FBXW7 is the substrate-recognition component of an SCF-type ubiquitin E3 ligase complex
                        responsible for ubiquitin-mediated proteasomal degradation of key oncoproteins including
                        c-MYC, cyclin E, NOTCH1/2, c-JUN, and MCL-1. The p.R505C variant affects the WD40 beta-
                        propeller domain critical for substrate binding. The arginine residues at positions 465,
                        479, and 505 form a mutational hotspot cluster in HNSCC and other solid tumors; substitution
                        at these positions impairs substrate ubiquitination, resulting in accumulation of growth-
                        promoting oncoproteins. While p.R505C is less functionally characterized than p.R465C or
                        p.R479Q, the structural homology and oncoproteome stabilization context support likely
                        loss-of-function. FBXW7 mutations occur in approximately 5-10% of HNSCC.
   Clinical Relevance:  Not directly actionable with currently available targeted agents. Stabilization of MCL-1
                        downstream of FBXW7 loss may theoretically affect sensitivity to BCL-2/BCL-XL inhibitors;
                        this remains investigational.
   Associated Pathways: Ubiquitin-proteasome system, cell cycle regulation (cyclin E), NOTCH signaling, MYC stability

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
NOTABLE NEGATIVE FINDINGS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

TP53:           No somatic mutation detected. This finding is EXPECTED and CONSISTENT with HPV-driven
                carcinogenesis: the HPV E6 oncoprotein mediates proteasomal degradation of wild-type p53
                protein, functionally inactivating the p53 pathway without requiring genomic mutation. The
                absence of TP53 mutation is a hallmark of HPV-positive HNSCC and distinguishes this tumor
                biologically from HPV-negative squamous cell carcinoma, in which TP53 mutation is present
                in >70% of cases.

CDKN2A/2B:     No homozygous deletion or point mutation detected. In HPV-positive HNSCC, the pRb pathway
                is functionally suppressed by the HPV E7 oncoprotein (which directly binds and inactivates
                pRb), obviating selective pressure for CDKN2A (p16) mutation or deletion. The strong
                nuclear/cytoplasmic p16 positivity on IHC (>70% of cells, block-positive pattern) reflects
                transcriptional upregulation of CDKN2A as a surrogate marker of active HPV E7 oncoprotein
                activity and is consistent with this molecular profile.

EGFR:          No amplification detected (copy number ratio 1.1). No activating point mutations in exons
                18-21 detected. EGFR amplification, when present in HNSCC, is more commonly associated with
                HPV-negative tumors. Absence of EGFR amplification is concordant with the HPV-positive
                molecular subtype.

FGFR1/2/3:     No FGFR1, FGFR2, or FGFR3 amplification detected. No FGFR fusion detected. No activating
                point mutations detected.

KRAS/NRAS/HRAS: No hotspot mutations detected in codons 12, 13, 61, or 146. RAS mutations are distinctly
                uncommon in HNSCC.

NOTCH1/2/3:    No pathogenic somatic alterations detected. NOTCH pathway alterations occur in a subset of
                HNSCC but were not identified in this specimen.

SOX2:          Focal gain at 3q26 locus detected (see copy number section); no point mutation.

PIK3R1:        No pathogenic mutation detected.

AKT1/2/3:      No activating mutations detected.

PTEN:          No deletion or inactivating mutation detected.

BRCA1/2:       No pathogenic somatic mutations detected. Germline BRCA analysis not performed on this
                report; if clinically indicated, reflex germline testing may be considered.

ATM:           No pathogenic somatic mutations detected.

NSD1:          No mutation detected. NSD1 mutations define a biologically distinct molecular subtype of
                HPV-negative HNSCC; its absence is concordant with HPV-positive status.

HRAS:          No mutation detected. HRAS mutations are more characteristic of oral cavity SCC.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
COPY NUMBER ANALYSIS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Significant Copy Number Alterations:

  Chromosome 3q Gain (focal, 3q25.1 - 3q29):
    Estimated copy number: 3-4 copies (low-level gain)
    Involved genes of note: PIK3CA (3q26.32), SOX2 (3q26.33), TP63 (3q28), TERC (3q26.2)
    Clinical significance: 3q gain is among the most recurrent cytogenomic events in HNSCC and head
    and neck precancerous lesions, reported in 40-65% of squamous cell carcinomas of the upper
    aerodigestive tract. Gain at 3q is an early event in squamous carcinogenesis and may contribute
    to amplification of PIK3CA (cooperating with the p.E545K hotspot mutation in this case) as well
    as overexpression of TP63 (deltaNp63 isoform), an important squamous differentiation and survival
    factor. SOX2 amplification at 3q also supports squamous lineage identity.

  No other focal amplifications or homozygous deletions of clinical significance detected.
  Genomic copy number profile otherwise within expected diploid range with focal alterations as noted.
  Loss of heterozygosity (LOH) analysis: Focal LOH on chromosome 9p21.3 (CDKN2A locus) detected by
  allelic imbalance, consistent with single-copy loss; no homozygous deletion. This may represent
  hemizygous deletion with retention of the wild-type allele, which is consistent with maintenance
  of p16 overexpression via E7-driven transcriptional upregulation.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
HPV ANALYSIS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

HPV Status:             POSITIVE — HIGH-RISK HPV DETECTED
HPV Genotype:           HPV-16 (most prevalent high-risk genotype in oropharyngeal SCC)
Detection Method:       Sequence reads mapping to HPV-16 genome identified within tumor sequencing data;
                        confirmed by RNA in situ hybridization (ISH) on the clinical pathology specimen
                        (reported separately, positive for high-risk HPV RNA)
Viral Integration:      DETECTED — Integration site identified at chromosome 8q24.21
                        (proximate to the MYC oncogene locus)
HPV Viral Load:         High (viral reads constitute approximately 3.2% of total tumor-mapping sequencing reads)
Integration Context:    HPV-16 integration at 8q24 is a recognized event in HPV-associated oropharyngeal SCC.
                        Integration near the MYC locus can disrupt local chromatin architecture and has been
                        associated with enhanced MYC transcriptional activity in some tumors, though copy number
                        gain of MYC itself was not detected in this specimen. The integration event results in
                        linearization of the viral episome and stable chromosomal incorporation, typically
                        associated with higher-level E6/E7 oncoprotein expression, p53 functional suppression,
                        and pRb pathway inactivation.
HPV Oncoproteins:       E6 expression inferred (functional p53 suppression consistent with absent TP53 mutation);
                        E7 expression inferred (functional pRb pathway suppression consistent with p16/CDKN2A
                        upregulation and absent CDKN2A deletion)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
BIOMARKER SUMMARY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Tumor Mutational Burden (TMB):
  Calculated TMB: 4 mutations/Mb — LOW (<10 mutations/Mb threshold for TMB-High designation)
  Methodology: Somatic nonsynonymous coding mutations per megabase of sequenced genome
  Context: HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC characteristically exhibits low TMB, consistent with
  viral-driven oncogenesis in which HPV oncoproteins (rather than mutagenic exposures such as tobacco
  carcinogens) are the primary transforming mechanism. Low TMB in HPV-positive HNSCC does not
  preclude benefit from immune checkpoint inhibition; PD-L1 expression and tumor-infiltrating
  lymphocyte (TIL) density are considered more relevant predictive biomarkers in this disease context.

Microsatellite Instability (MSI):
  MSI Status: MICROSATELLITE STABLE (MSS)
  Loci analyzed: 30 microsatellite loci (17 mononucleotide, 13 dinucleotide repeats)
  Unstable loci: 0 of 30 (0%)
  Context: MSS is the expected finding in squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck; MSI-High
  is exceedingly rare in HNSCC (<1% of cases). No deficiency mismatch repair (dMMR) detected.

PD-L1 Expression:
  CPS (Combined Positive Score): 18 (by external IHC send-out, Dako 22C3 antibody, reported separately)
  Interpretation: CPS >= 1 (PD-L1 positive by FDA-approved threshold for pembro in HNSCC
  recurrent/metastatic setting). CPS 18 reflects moderate PD-L1 expression on tumor cells and
  tumor-infiltrating immune cells. Contextually relevant if systemic therapy for recurrent or
  metastatic disease is indicated.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
FUSION / STRUCTURAL VARIANT ANALYSIS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

No targetable gene fusions detected.
No NTRK1/2/3 fusions identified.
No RET fusions identified.
No ALK fusions identified.
No ROS1 fusions identified.
No FGFR2/3 fusions identified.
Structural rearrangement at HPV-16 integration site (chromosome 8q24.21) detected as described above.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
GERMLINE DISCLAIMER
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

This report reflects somatic tumor sequencing with matched normal (peripheral blood) analysis.
All variants reported herein have been confirmed as somatic (absent in matched normal/germline DNA).
No germline pathogenic variants were identified in cancer predisposition genes on the matched normal
analysis; however, this assay is not designed or validated for clinical germline reporting. If germline
cancer predisposition testing is clinically indicated (e.g., family history, early age of onset,
multiple primaries), referral to genetic counseling and dedicated germline testing is recommended.
No incidental germline findings of clinical significance were flagged during somatic/normal comparison.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
QUALITY METRICS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Metric                          Tumor         Normal (Blood)
Mean on-target coverage:        742x          312x
Percent bases >100x coverage:   97.3%         94.8%
Duplication rate:               6.4%          8.1%
Transition/transversion ratio:  2.12          N/A (tumor)
Tumor purity (estimated):       55%
LOD (limit of detection):       ~3% VAF at standard coverage
All quality thresholds met; report is analytically valid.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
INTEGRATED MOLECULAR INTERPRETATION
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

This tumor demonstrates a molecular profile highly characteristic of HPV-positive oropharyngeal
squamous cell carcinoma. Key molecular features include:

(1) HPV-16 as the primary oncogenic driver, with confirmed chromosomal integration at 8q24, high
    viral load, and functional inactivation of the p53 and pRb tumor suppressor pathways via E6/E7
    oncoproteins (corroborated by the absence of TP53 mutation and absence of CDKN2A deletion,
    with strong p16 overexpression by IHC).

(2) PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway activation via PIK3CA p.E545K hotspot mutation (VAF 18%) occurring in
    the context of 3q amplification — a recurrent and co-occurring alteration in HPV-positive HNSCC
    that may be relevant if targeted therapy is considered in a recurrent/metastatic scenario.

(3) Immune pathway dysregulation via TRAF3 loss-of-function (p.R118*, VAF 22%), activating
    non-canonical NF-kappaB signaling, a pathway selectively enriched in HPV-positive HNSCC and
    potentially relevant to innate immune evasion and inflammatory tumor microenvironment remodeling.

(4) Chromatin remodeling pathway co-mutation involving both KMT2D (histone H3K4 methyltransferase,
    truncating, VAF 19%) and EP300 (histone acetyltransferase, missense, VAF 15%), reflecting a
    commonly disrupted epigenetic regulation axis in HNSCC.

(5) FBXW7 p.R505C (VAF 12%) — ubiquitin-mediated proteolysis pathway alteration with potential
    downstream effects on c-MYC, NOTCH, and cyclin E stability.

Overall genomic landscape: Low tumor mutational burden (4 mut/Mb), microsatellite stable, consistent
with a virally driven carcinogenesis mechanism. PD-L1 CPS of 18 may be relevant for immune checkpoint
inhibitor eligibility in the recurrent/metastatic setting. No high-confidence targetable alterations
beyond PIK3CA are identified; the PIK3CA p.E545K mutation warrants consideration for PI3K-directed
clinical trial enrollment if disease recurrence occurs.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
METHODS NOTE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

DNA was extracted from FFPE tissue following macrodissection of tumor-enriched regions by a
board-certified pathologist. Hybrid capture-based targeted sequencing was performed using a
proprietary 500-gene panel encompassing all coding exons of cancer-relevant genes, select
intronic regions for fusion detection, and microsatellite repeat loci. Sequencing was performed
on an Illumina NovaSeq 6000 platform. Bioinformatic analysis was performed using validated
pipelines for somatic SNV/indel calling (paired tumor-normal), CNV analysis (log2 ratio and
allelic imbalance), fusion detection, MSI quantification, and TMB calculation. Variants were
filtered against population databases (gnomAD, ExAC) and classified per AMP/ASCO/CAP guidelines
(2017, updated). All variants with VAF >5% and minimum 10 supporting reads were reviewed manually
by a molecular pathologist.
